serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bento box portion is approximately 350 g, comprising a serving of steamed white rice, grilled or pan-seared scallops, and teriyaki-glazed chicken, typically accompanied by a small side of vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Scallop+Teriyaki Chicken Bento 带子+鸡扒饭盒 — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Scallop+Teriyaki Chicken Bento (cr_id G00010132). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable scallop and teriyaki chicken preparations.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for scallop-and-teriyaki-chicken bento. The dish contains very low carbohydrate (1.62 g/100g) from the protein-dominant components; however, the bento is typically served with steamed white rice. The GI of 54 is estimated using the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed protein-and-rice meal analogues (sushi/rice-based Japanese meals, GI ~54–56). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (54 × 5.67) / 100 ≈ 3.1 for the protein components alone; when accounting for a standard 100 g rice accompaniment (carbohydrate ~28 g, GI 72), blended GL per 350 g serving ≈ 4.9. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with direct measurement data when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 723 mg/100g — substantially exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to sodium at 723 mg/100g driven by teriyaki sauce; if consumed, request reduced or no teriyaki sauce and avoid pickled vegetable sides to lower total sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (1.62 g/100g) and sugar (1.47 g/100g) in the protein components; high protein (25.73 g/100g) supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: The scallop and chicken components are very low in carbohydrate and sugar, making them suitable for blood glucose management; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor the rice portion size carefully, as a full rice serving will substantially increase the glycaemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (723 mg/100g) raises cardiovascular risk; moderate cholesterol (105 mg/100g) and relatively low saturated fat (~1.4 g/100g) are partially offsetting factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (723 mg/100g) is the primary cardiovascular concern and warrants caution for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; the lean protein profile of scallops and chicken is favourable, but the teriyaki sauce sodium load should be minimised by requesting reduced sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 723 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA threshold; absence of meaningful omega-3 fatty acids limits anti-inflammatory benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in standard preparation due to sodium at 723 mg/100g, which exceeds the RA-specific 400 mg/100g threshold and may worsen tissue swelling; scallops provide lean protein but lack the omega-3 anti-inflammatory potency of fatty fish such as salmon or mackerel.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Scallop and Teriyaki Chicken Bento (带子+鸡扒饭盒) is a Japanese-influenced bento box dish widely available in Singapore food courts and hawker centres. The dish combines pan-seared or grilled scallops with teriyaki-glazed chicken (typically chicken thigh or breast), served alongside steamed white rice and a small portion of vegetables. The teriyaki glaze — a sweet-savoury sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar — imparts a caramelised, umami-rich coating to the chicken, while the scallops contribute a delicate, slightly sweet oceanic flavour and a tender, lightly firm texture. Nutritionally, the protein components are exceptionally high in protein (25.73 g/100g) and relatively low in fat (5.96 g/100g) and carbohydrate (1.62 g/100g), making them favourable for lean protein intake. However, sodium is elevated at 723 mg/100g due to the teriyaki sauce, which is a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. Cholesterol is moderate at 105 mg/100g. The dish is not suitable for hypertension without sauce reduction. It is well-suited for high-protein dietary goals and is broadly popular as a lunch or dinner option in Singapore's Japanese food court segment.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010132, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. The dish identity is exact. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogues and documented accordingly.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.4 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable scallop and teriyaki chicken preparations (USDA FDC #175177 sea scallops and #174608 chicken thigh, teriyaki-style); HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific composite bento dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB — confirmed zero per the HPB source record. 4. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for mixed protein-and-rice Japanese meal analogues; direct GI measurement for this specific dish is not available. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; teriyaki sauce may contain mirin (alcohol) and the dish is not certified halal. 6. The dish name retains the '+' separator from the original HPB record as a recognised alias form.
